questions.q: What does PCA do?
options: Split the data into a training set and a test set
Squeeze many columns into a few, keeping most information
Add brand-new randomly-generated columns to the dataset
Label every row so the model can learn from them
questions.explain: PCA reduces dimensions while keeping most of the variance (information).

questions.q: PC1 (the first principal component) is:
options: The answer column that the model tries to predict
The single first column in the original dataset
The average value taken across all of the columns
The direction where the data spreads out the most
questions.explain: PC1 points where the data varies most — it holds the most information.

questions.q: Are PCA's components your original columns?
options: No, they are simply the original labels copied over
Yes, it keeps some columns and deletes the others
No, they are new columns made by mixing the originals
Yes, the same columns but with shorter new names
questions.explain: PCA is feature extraction: new blended components, not a subset of columns.

questions.q: Why must you scale before PCA?
options: Because scaling removes all outliers from the data
Because the library will crash without scaling first
Because scaling makes the training run much faster
Because big-range columns would otherwise dominate
questions.explain: Unscaled large-range features look like all the variance, so PCA follows only them.

questions.q: `PCA(n_components=0.95)` means:
options: Keep enough components to retain 95% of variance
Throw away ninety-five percent of all the features
Keep exactly ninety-five components from the data
Set the model's confidence level to ninety-five
questions.explain: A value 0–1 = fraction of information to keep; PCA picks how many that needs.

questions.q: In PCA, 'variance' roughly means:
options: The size of each step the model takes downhill
How spread out and different the data points are
How accurate the final trained model will be
The total number of rows inside the dataset
questions.explain: High variance = points differ a lot in that direction = information.

questions.q: Does PCA use the label (y)?
options: No, it only looks at the features and ignores y
Only when you choose to run it in supervised mode
Yes, it needs the labels to reduce dimensions
Only when the data happens to be made of images
questions.explain: PCA is unsupervised — features only, ignores the label.

questions.q: A main reason to use PCA is:
options: To create labels for data that has none yet
To divide the dataset into train and test parts
To increase the number of columns in the data
To view high-dimensional data and speed up models
questions.explain: Fewer cleaner columns → you can plot and models run faster.

questions.q: t-SNE and UMAP are mainly used for:
options: Training a classifier to predict the labels
Cleaning the missing values out of the dataset
Splitting the dataset into train and test parts
Drawing high-dimensional data as a 2D picture
questions.explain: They squeeze many columns into 2 so you can see clusters.

questions.q: Should you feed t-SNE output into a model?
options: Yes, feeding it always improves the accuracy
No, it is for looking only; its distances are distorted
Yes, as long as you remember to scale the data first
Yes, but only for regression-type problems
questions.explain: t-SNE distorts distances — for the picture, not for modelling.

questions.q: t-SNE mainly preserves:
options: Local structure: nearby points stay close together
The size of the file that the data came from
The global layout but never the local structure
The labels that are attached to each of the points
questions.explain: t-SNE keeps local neighbourhoods intact.

questions.q: If your data has only 2 columns, do you need t-SNE?
options: Yes, because every dataset needs t-SNE first
Yes, but only if you have access to a fast GPU
No, you can just plot the two columns directly
Yes, t-SNE is required before any plotting
questions.explain: With 2 columns, plot them straight — t-SNE is for many columns.

questions.q: UMAP compared to t-SNE is usually:
options: Always less accurate no matter what the data is
Much slower than t-SNE on the same dataset
Exactly the same code as simply running PCA
Usually faster and keeps the big picture better
questions.explain: UMAP is generally faster and often keeps global structure better.

questions.q: How do t-SNE and matplotlib relate?
options: They compete to do exactly the same job
t-SNE shrinks columns to 2, then matplotlib draws them
matplotlib fully replaces the need for using t-SNE
They are really just two names for one single tool
questions.explain: t-SNE makes data drawable; matplotlib draws it — together.

questions.q: 50 columns and you want to SEE the data. Best move?
options: Use t-SNE or UMAP to get 2 columns, then plot
Just plot the first column against the second one
Train a much bigger model on all of the columns
Delete forty-eight of the columns at random first
questions.explain: 2 raw columns hide the rest; t-SNE uses all of them, then plot.

questions.q: Distances between clusters in a t-SNE plot are:
options: Exact and safe to measure with a ruler
The same number as the model's accuracy score
Always equal to exactly zero between groups
Not reliable, so you should not measure them
questions.explain: The squeezing distorts distances, so gaps aren't trustworthy.

questions.q: An anomaly is:
options: A point that appears very often throughout the dataset
Just another name for the label of a row
A rare point that is different from the normal ones
The exact average point of the whole dataset
questions.explain: Anomaly = the rare odd one out, unlike normal data.

questions.q: Why does a normal classifier struggle with fraud?
options: Fraud is far too easy for a model to spot
It always needs an expensive GPU in order to run
Fraud is actually never rare in real-world data
Few fraud cases, few labels, and brand-new patterns
questions.explain: Fraud is rare, has few labels, and new tricks have no examples.

questions.q: The main idea of unsupervised anomaly detection is:
options: Always predict that every single point is fraud
Delete every rare row from the dataset first
Learn exactly what fraud looks like in fine detail
Learn what normal looks like, then flag the odd ones
questions.explain: We have lots of normal data → learn it, flag what differs.

questions.q: Isolation Forest flags anomalies by:
options: The overall accuracy of the trained model
The distance from the point to a centroid
The importance score that each input feature receives
How few random cuts are needed to isolate a point
questions.explain: Odd points isolate in very few random cuts. Few cuts = anomaly.

questions.q: In Isolation Forest, a label of -1 means:
options: A perfectly normal and average data point
The single best data point in the dataset
An anomaly, while a label of 1 means normal
A value that is simply missing from the row
questions.explain: -1 = anomaly/outlier, 1 = normal (inlier).

questions.q: `contamination=0.01` means:
options: Keep just one percent of the columns of data
The model reaches one percent of accuracy
Only one anomaly exists in the whole dataset
You expect about one percent to be anomalies
questions.explain: contamination sets the cut — flags the weirdest ~1%.

questions.q: A model that always predicts 'normal' on 0.1% fraud is:
options: About 99.9% accurate but it catches no fraud
A perfect model that catches all of the fraud
Something that is simply impossible to ever build
A model that is extremely slow when it runs
questions.explain: Accuracy lies on rare events: high accuracy, zero fraud caught.

questions.q: LOF (Local Outlier Factor) detects an anomaly by:
options: Seeing if a point is lonely while neighbours crowd
Squaring the error between the guess and truth
Making many random cuts through the dataset
Reading the label that came with each point
questions.explain: LOF compares a point's local density to its neighbours'.

questions.q: What does one neuron do?
options: Only store a single number to use later on
Multiply inputs by weights, add up, then activate
Delete the columns that are not very useful
Sort all of the data from low values to high
questions.explain: A neuron: multiply × weights → add → activate → output.

questions.q: During training, what does a neural network learn?
options: The size of the step it takes going downhill
The weights that make its guesses better
The total number of rows inside the dataset
The names of the files that it was given
questions.explain: Training adjusts the weights to improve the guesses.

questions.q: Deep learning is:
options: A spreadsheet program for very large tables
A special kind of database for very big data
A neural network that has many hidden layers
A method used for clustering unlabeled points
questions.explain: Deep learning = neural networks with many layers.

questions.q: A neural network is part of:
options: Databases that are used to store the records
Statistics and absolutely nothing else at all
The computer's main operating system itself
Machine learning, like trees and K-Means
questions.explain: A neural network is one type of ML model.

questions.q: What is an activation function for?
options: Deleting the neurons that are not being used
Splitting the dataset into separate train and test sets
Attaching a label to each of the data rows
Adding non-linearity so it learns complex patterns
questions.explain: Without activation the network is just arithmetic.

questions.q: A typical neural network is:
options: Something that is used only for clustering
Always unsupervised with no labels at all
Usually supervised, because it needs labels
It is a kind of model that is never trained
questions.explain: Most NNs are supervised — they learn from labels.

questions.q: The sentence vector [1,1,0,1,0] means:
options: The loss value for this one single sentence
The weights the network has learned so far
Which words are present (1) or missing (0)
The overall accuracy of the trained model
questions.explain: Bag-of-words: 1 = word present, 0 = not present.

questions.q: Adding more layers generally lets a network:
options: Learn more complex patterns, but needs more data
Run a great deal faster than a small network
Turn the whole network into an unsupervised one
Use far fewer weights than it did before
questions.explain: More layers = more capacity, at the cost of more data.

questions.q: The loss (the 'oops score') measures:
options: The size of the step taken on each update
How fast the training process is going along
The total number of layers in the network
How wrong the model's current guesses are
questions.explain: Loss = one number for how wrong the model is now.

questions.q: Gradient descent does:
options: Squish a number into the zero-to-one range
Split the dataset into train and test parts
Add a lot more training data to the dataset
Step the weights downhill to lower the loss
questions.explain: It nudges weights toward lower loss — walking downhill.

questions.q: Backpropagation does:
options: Load the dataset into the computer's memory
Draw the charts of the training curves
Work out how much each weight is to blame
Choose which optimizer the model will use
questions.explain: Backprop finds each weight's share of the error (gradients).

questions.q: The learning rate is:
options: The number of epochs used during training
The final accuracy the model reaches
The size of each step when updating weights
The loss value at the very end of training
questions.explain: Learning rate = how big a step you take downhill.

questions.q: If the learning rate is too BIG:
options: Absolutely nothing about the training changes
The loss overshoots and bounces or blows up
Accuracy instantly jumps to one hundred percent
Training simply becomes a little bit slower
questions.explain: Too-big steps overshoot the valley and can blow up.

questions.q: Which optimizer is the common default to start with?
options: DBSCAN, which is the density-based method
K-Means, which is the clustering algorithm
Adam, which mixes momentum and adaptive steps
Plain SGD with no tuning of any kind at all
questions.explain: Adam is the usual go-to default optimizer.

questions.q: The sigmoid function squashes a number into the range:
options: The range from zero up to one hundred
The range from negative one up to one
The range from 0 to 1
Any number, with no upper limit at all
questions.explain: Sigmoid squashes any input into 0–1 (a probability).

questions.q: ReLU does what to a NEGATIVE number?
options: Doubles the value to make it much bigger
Leaves the number exactly as it already was
Changes the number so it becomes a one
Turns it into 0
questions.explain: ReLU = max(0, x): negatives become 0, positives stay.
